subject: Re: Crash on subtomogram export
body:
Warning: what follows *could* be construed as a rant.

Okay!
I have reimported my tilt series and redone CTF estimation.
The high–phase shift tilt series once again caused problems, so I am 
discarding it.
However, this did not immediately make it possible to extract all my 
subtomos without Warp crashing.
What I have resorted to is to split my particles.star file by tilt series 
and to run Warp's subtomo extraction on each subset in sequence.
This is *really slow*.
It takes me a long time to click around the GUI, and it takes Warp a long 
time to start actually doing work.
Also, I have to close and reopen Warp between runs because the *Volumes* check 
box is sometimes unclickable.
The situation with "flat" subtomos (those full of zeros) is marginally 
worse now: of my 22 tilt series, 8 give flat subtomos.
I have yet to find any reliable predictor of which tilt series Warp will 
make flat subtomos from.
However, I have found that removing those 8 tilt series allows Warp to 
extract — without issue — the remaining particles (a sad 66% of my original 
set).
Unfortunately, the only way to know which tilt series Warp will crash on is 
to undertake the painstaking per–tilt series process I have just detailed.

I realise that this message may come across as overly critical.
Sorry about that.
Warp is great: when it *does* produce (sensible) subtomos, they average 
beautifully.
I eagerly anticipate future updates.

Best,
James

On Thursday, August 3, 2023 at 3:33:00 PM UTC+1 James Hooker wrote:

> Hi, me again.
>
> I have identified the offending tilt series, and it seems the issue was 
> caused by a large phase shift estimated in the CTF (1.18 pi).
> There appears to be no way to limit the range of phase shifts that Warp 
> considers.
> I guess my options are to redo CTF estimation (perhaps without phase shift 
> estimation) or to throw out this tilt series.
>
> On a separate note, 7/23 of my tilt series give subtomograms full of zeros.
> I have seen mention of this problem elsewhere in this group.
> Not sure what is causing it in this instance.
>
> Best,
> James
>
> On Tuesday, August 1, 2023 at 4:28:54 PM UTC+1 James Hooker wrote:
>
>> Yep, I'm now splitting the particles by tilt series in order to narrow 
>> down where the trouble is. Quite tedious clicking through the GUI. We'll 
>> see.
>>
>> On Tuesday, August 1, 2023 at 4:19:56 PM UTC+1 cla...@gmail.com wrote:
>>
>>> Hi James,
>>>
>>> The problem most likely lies with a specific tilt series (or particle) 
>>> and as a quick check you could remove the troublesome tomogram's particles 
>>> from the star file and try again. I've had a similar issue where my 
>>> particle coordinates were outside the tomogram (i.e. the star file 
>>> contained negative numbers). You can look in the subtomo folder at the 
>>> particles being extracted and see if it stops mid-way during a tomogram 
>>> extraction (the next particle will be your problem) and check your star 
>>> file for anything odd related to that particle.
>>>
>>> Cheers,
>>>
>>> Mat
>>>
>>> On Tue, 1 Aug 2023 at 15:22, James Hooker <jho...@mrc-lmb.cam.ac.uk> 
>>> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Hello all,
>>>>
>>>> I'm trying to export some subtomograms, but after Warp completes the 
>>>> boxes for 1-and-a-bit tilt series, it announces that it has crashed. One of 
>>>> its threads keeps writing out subtomograms for quite a while, but not all 
>>>> of them, and no star file gets written. Is this something anyone has seen 
>>>> before?
>>>>
>>>> Best,
>>>> James
>>>>
>>>> --

>>>> .
>>>>
>>>
